message.content:
You are helping design the P0 phase of a brand-new, currently-empty monorepo called "flowfig". The repo right now contains only a README.md — there is no existing code, no package.json, no tsconfig. Do not spend time searching for existing patterns; there are none. This is greenfield design work. Read the repo yourself at /workspaces/flowfig to confirm the empty state (just README.md) before designing.

## Product context

flowfig bridges two producers of UI (an existing-page-capturing Chrome extension, and an agent-kit CLI that renders agent-generated code via Playwright) into a single JSON file format, which a Figma plugin's "Node Writer" consumes to create/update real Figma nodes. Full architecture is below — read it carefully, it contains binding decisions:

---
Two producers, one translation bridge, one writer. Existing pages get captured by a Chrome extension. Generated code gets compiled by an installable CLI. Both land on the same file format, and only one module — the Figma plugin's Node Writer — is ever allowed to touch figma.*.

THE THREE FILE KINDS (this is the target schema P0 must define):
| File | Produced by | Contains | Consumed by |
| *.capture.flowfig.json | extension (resolve() only) | Resolved-but-untranslated tree — raw arbitrary CSS | plugin: full translate + fuzzy-match + write |
| *.translated.flowfig.json | agent-kit (resolve() + translate()) | Already-translated node spec, with tagged component/token refs | plugin: key-resolution + write only |
| .flowfig/symbols.json | plugin's scraper | Symbol table — components/variables/styles scraped live | agent-kit + the agent itself, read off disk |

TAGGING CONVENTION (source markup the translator must recognize):
<div data-flowfig-component="a1b2c3" data-flowfig-props='{"size":"lg"}'>  — whole-component reference, resolved by key
<div data-flowfig-new-component="CardHeader">  — explicitly new, writer creates it and writes the key back
.price { background: var(--ffig-color-accent-500); }  — token-level reference, CSS custom property naming convention --ffig-*, survives cascade because the CLI reads declared CSS source, not just computed output

NODE WRITER RESOLUTION (informs what fields the translated schema needs, even though Node Writer itself is built in P1, not P0):
- Key resolution (tagged): deterministic bind/instantiate by key looked up live via Plugin API.
- Fuzzy matching (untagged): exact match binds a Variable/Style silently by default (this is a user-toggleable plugin setting — default auto-bind-exact, toggle to always-confirm); close match surfaces as suggestion; no match is a per-run user choice (literal value or mint new token).
- Tagged componentRef has 3 outcomes: resolves in open file (bind/instantiate); known key but missing from this file (surface to user, don't guess); explicitly flagged new (Writer creates component, writes fresh key back into symbol table — mirror runs both directions).

CSS RESOLUTION — WHY THE TWO PRODUCERS DIFFER (this shapes what the resolver's INPUT contract must look like, since it has to serve both producers uniformly):
The extension runs inside a real rendered tab, so getComputedStyle()/getBoundingClientRect() give it ground truth for free. It only needs core/resolver's normalization step and core/format; it never bundles core/translator, since capture files are translated later, inside the plugin (capture files are resolved but UNtranslated).
agent-kit has no browser, so it depends on Playwright (hard dependency from day one, chosen over jsdom/static-parse because it gives a real layout engine — true layout for non-auto-layout content and intrinsic sizing that jsdom/static CSS parsing can't produce). agent-kit's pipeline is resolve() THEN translate() — both steps, producing an already-translated file.

REPO LAYOUT (already decided, described in the architecture doc — you should follow this shape):
flowfig/
├── package.json            # npm workspaces: "packages/*"
├── tsconfig.base.json
├── packages/
│   ├── core/                # private — resolver, translator, design-system matching. NEVER published, never leaves the monorepo, exists only for clean cross-bundler imports (each downstream package's own bundler — esbuild via @create-figma-plugin for figma-plugin, some CLI bundler for agent-kit, webpack/esbuild for the MV3 extension — needs to consume core cleanly, targeting its OWN runtime, so core should NOT prescribe a single compiled target)
│   ├── figma-plugin/         # P1 — not built yet, stub only for P0
│   ├── extension/            # P3 — not built yet, stub only for P0
│   └── agent-kit/            # P2 — not built yet, stub only for P0
├── examples/                 # shared fixtures — keeps the two adapters honest against one contract (both extension and agent-kit resolver output must satisfy the same fixture-tested contract)
├── docs/                     # ships to consumers, incl. agent-kit's generated AGENT-GUIDE.md (populated in P2, placeholder for now)
└── Documentation/            # dev-only, already contains architecture-plan.md

MVP ROADMAP — P0 IS THE ONLY PHASE IN SCOPE RIGHT NOW:
P0 | Core engine skeleton | Resolver + Translator as pure, fixture-tested TS packages — no browser or Figma runtime needed to validate the schema.
(P1 figma-plugin, P2 agent-kit, P3 extension, P4 parity depth, P5 fidelity polish all come LATER — do not design their internals, just leave clean seams for them.)

Already-decided tooling/process constraints (do not re-litigate these, they are final):
- npm workspaces (not pnpm/yarn)
- formatVersion field on every file, hard-reject on mismatch (no migration shims)
- figma-plugin will use @create-figma-plugin later (P1) — irrelevant to P0 but means core's build output must be consumable by esbuild-based bundling eventually
- Snippet storage, fuzzy-match-setting, capture-scope-includes-structural-matching are settled decisions for LATER phases (P1/P3), not relevant to P0's job

## What you need to design (P0 only)

1. **Monorepo scaffold**: root package.json (workspaces config, scripts), tsconfig.base.json (strict mode, target/module choices that work for a package meant to be consumed by three very different downstream bundlers — Figma plugin sandbox, Node CLI, browser extension), test runner choice (recommend one: Vitest vs Jest, justify briefly), and whether core ships as compiled dist+d.ts or is consumed as raw TS source via workspace protocol by every downstream bundler (recommend one, justify against the "clean cross-bundler imports" requirement above).

2. **packages/core internal structure**: concrete src/ layout for format/, resolver/, translator/ modules. For each, define:
   - format/: the actual TypeScript types for FlowfigNode (recursive tree — needs at minimum: node type discriminant like frame/text/vector/image, geometry/box, layout-mode e.g. autoLayout mapping from flex/grid, a style/props bag, text content+runs for text nodes, children), ComponentRef, TokenRef, the CaptureFile vs TranslatedFile file-level wrapper shape (formatVersion + root + metadata), and formatVersion constant/validation.
   - resolver/: the pure function(s) that normalize a generic "computed style + geometry + DOM structure" snapshot (something BOTH getComputedStyle-in-a-real-tab AND Playwright's page.evaluate can produce in the same shape) into a resolved FlowfigNode tree. Define the resolver's INPUT type precisely — this is the contract both producers must satisfy.
   - translator/: the pure function(s) that take a resolved tree + the data-flowfig-* tag info (design how tags survive from source DOM into the resolver's input/output so translator can read them) + a symbol-table shape (stub/mock shape is fine, real scraper is P1) and produce a TranslatedFile with componentRef/newComponent annotations and CSS custom-property token refs (--ffig-* convention) swapped in for literal style values.

3. **examples/ fixtures approach**: what a fixture looks like (e.g. paired input DOM/computed-style snapshot + expected resolved JSON + expected translated JSON) and how core's tests consume them, such that figma-plugin/agent-kit/extension can later reuse the SAME fixtures to stay honest against the one contract.

4. **Concrete file list** for P0: every file to create, in order, with a one-line purpose each. Include root config files, packages/core/*, packages/{figma-plugin,extension,agent-kit}/package.json stubs (just enough to be valid npm workspace members, no real code), examples/ fixture files, and a docs/ placeholder.

5. **Verification plan**: how to prove P0 works end to end — what test command to run, what it should assert (fixture-in → resolved-out and resolved-in → translated-out matching expected JSON), and any typecheck/build command.

Do NOT design P1-P5 internals. Do NOT pick a Playwright integration yet (that's P2). Focus entirely on making core/format, core/resolver, core/translator real, tested, and provably correct against fixtures — that's what "no browser or Figma runtime needed to validate the schema" means.

Return a detailed, concrete implementation plan: file-by-file, with key type definitions written out in full (not hand-waved), a recommended test framework and monorepo build strategy with justification, and the verification command(s) to run.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact page can be granted — behavior static HTML cannot provide on its own, such as the page reading live or connected data, keeping state shared across viewers, or updating and republishing itself. Serves this user's live capability roster and the typed call definitions. Load it whenever the user asks for an artifact needing any such runtime behavior.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.command: ls -la && find . -maxdepth 3 -not -path './.git*' | sort && echo "---README---" && cat README.md 2>/dev/null && echo "---Documentation---" && find Documentation -type f 2>/dev/null

drwxrwxrwx+ 2 codespace codespace 4096 Jul 18 08:17 Documentation
-rw-rw-rw-  1 codespace root        71 Jul 18 08:02 README.md

---README---
# flowfig
figma plugin that integrates AI development tools with figma
---Documentation---
Documentation/architecture-plan.md

1 # flowfig — Architecture Plan 2 3 Two producers, one translation bridge, one writer. Existing pages get **captured** by a Chrome extension. Generated code gets **compiled** by an installable CLI. Both land on the same file format, and only one module — the Figma plugin's Node Writer — is ever allowed to touch `figma.*`. 4 5 Same branding family as flowkit — **no functional relationship** — but the same DNA: one repo, several independently-shipped surfaces, a shared core underneath, and a spec that teaches AI agents the local conventions. 6 7 --- 8 9 ## Pipeline overview 10 11 ``` 12 Producer A — existing pages Producer B — generated code 13 ───────────────────────────── ───────────────────────────── 14 │ Chrome extension │ │ agent-kit CLI │ 15 │ real live DOM → │ │ Playwright-rendered → │ 16 │ getComputedStyle(), │ │ real layout engine, no │ 17 │ getBoundingClientRect() │ │ browser tab needed │ 18 ──────────────┬────────────── ──────────────┬────────────── 19 │ resolve() │ resolve() + translate() 20 ▼ ▼ 21 ───────────────────────────── ───────────────────────────── 22 │ *.capture.flowfig.json │ │ *.translated.flowfig.json │ 23 │ resolved, untranslated → │ │ tagged refs already │ 24 │ arbitrary CSS, no tag │ │ resolved to component/ │ 25 │ guarantees │ │ token keys │ 26 ──────────────┬────────────── ──────────────┬────────────── 27 └─────────────────────┬──────────────────────────┘ 28 ▼ 29 ──────────────────────────────────────── 30 │ Figma plugin — the only figma.* caller │ 31 │ • Import — accepts either file kind │ 32 │ • Scrape — components/variables/ │ 33 │ styles → symbol table │ 34 │ • Node Writer — key-resolve or fuzzy- │ 35 │ match, then write │ 36 ──────────────────────────────────────── 37 │ 38 └─▶ exports .flowfig/symbols.json, 39 read back by agent-kit for tagging — 40 the mirror runs in both directions 41 ``` 42 43 --- 44 45 ## The three file kinds 46 47 Everything in this system moves as a file hand-off, never a live connection — consistent end to end, from capture to symbol-table export. 48 49 | File | Produced by | Contains | Consumed by | 50 |---|---|---|---| 51 | `*.capture.flowfig.json` | extension (`resolve()` only) | Resolved-but-untranslated tree — raw arbitrary CSS | plugin: full translate + fuzzy-match + write | 52 | `*.translated.flowfig.json` | agent-kit (`resolve()` + `translate()`) | Already-translated node spec, with tagged component/token refs | plugin: key-resolution + write only | 53 | `.flowfig/symbols.json` | plugin's scraper | Symbol table — components/variables/styles scraped live | agent-kit + the agent itself, read off disk | 54 55 --- 56 57 ## Tagging convention 58 59 The agent has the symbol table on disk before it generates anything, so it can reference real Figma objects by their stable key — not a name that can drift. 60 61 ```html 62 63
64 65 66
67 68 /* token-level reference — survives cascade because the CLI reads 69 declared CSS source, not just computed output */ 70 .price { background: var(--ffig-color-accent-500); } 71 ``` 72 73 Untagged elements and values still work — they just fall back to the same fuzzy-matching parity engine the extension's arbitrary captures rely on. Tagging isn't all-or-nothing per file. 74 75 --- 76 77 ## Node Writer — two resolution modes, side by side 78 79 - **Key resolution** (tagged) — deterministic. Look up the key live via the Plugin API and bind/instantiate. No ambiguity. 80 - **Fuzzy matching** (untagged) — exact match binds a Variable/Style; close match surfaces as a suggestion; no match is a per-run user choice (literal value or mint a new token). 81 82 Three outcomes when a tagged `componentRef` is hit: 83 84 1. **Resolves in the open file** — bind/instantiate. Happy path. 85 2. **Known key, missing from this file** — surface to the user rather than guess. Also how a stale symbol-table export gets caught — the Writer always re-verifies live, so staleness only ever bites the agent's tags, never the write itself. 86 3. **Explicitly flagged as new** — Writer creates the component from the translated subtree, registers it in the file, and **writes the fresh key back into the symbol table**. The mirror runs both directions: Figma → code by scrape, code → Figma → code by this write-back. 87 88 ### Fuzzy-match confirmation is a user setting, not a hardcoded default 89 90 Whether an *exact* match auto-binds silently or still surfaces for confirmation is a plugin setting, not fixed behavior: 91 92 - **Default: auto-bind exact matches silently.** An exact match to a Variable/Style has no ambiguity — it gets the same no-friction treatment as tagged key-resolution. The confirmation list is reserved for close-but-not-exact matches, where there's a genuine judgment call. 93 - **Toggle: confirm everything.** Users who want to review every binding before it's written — including exact matches — can flip this on. Slower per-import, but nothing lands without explicit sign-off. 94 95 This setting lives in plugin `clientStorage` alongside snippet storage (below). 96 97 --- 98 99 ## Snippet storage 100 101 Snippets (captured or generated) are stored in the Figma plugin's `clientStorage` only — no sync service, no account system. A snippet made on one machine is not insertable from another machine. This matches the rest of the system's file-hand-off philosophy: nothing here depends on a hosted backend, and it keeps the plugin's only state surface local and simple. Revisit if cross-machine reuse becomes a real user request — it would need a hosted service and auth that no other part of this architecture currently needs. 102 103 --- 104 105 ## Publish matrix 106 107 Four workspace packages. Only one of them ever runs `npm publish` — the other three ship somewhere else entirely, or nowhere. 108 109 | Package | Ships via | Notes | 110 |---|---|---| 111 | `agent-kit` | **npm** | The flagship, installable CLI. Any project can add it and generate Figma files straight from its own code. | 112 | `figma-plugin` | Figma Community | Reviewed and distributed by Figma, not npm. Importer, scraper, Node Writer. Built on `@create-figma-plugin` (see Decisions). | 113 | `extension` | Chrome Web Store | Reviewed and distributed by Google, not npm. Live-page capture only. | 114 | `core` | *private* | Never published, never leaves the monorepo. Exists only for clean cross-bundler imports. | 115 116 --- 117 118 ## Repo layout 119 120 ``` 121 flowfig/ 122 ├── package.json # npm workspaces: "packages/*" 123 ├── tsconfig.base.json 124 ├── packages/ 125 │ ├── core/ # private — resolver, translator, design-system matching 126 │ ├── figma-plugin/ # manifest.json (Figma's) + main/ui + scraper + Node Writer 127 │ ├── extension/ # manifest.json (Chrome MV3) + content script + popup 128 │ └── agent-kit/ # the one npm-published package — CLI + tagging + agent guide 129 ├── examples/ # shared fixtures — keeps the two adapters honest against one contract 130 ├── docs/ # ships to consumers — incl. agent-kit's generated AGENT-GUIDE.md 131 └── Documentation/ # dev-only — architecture rationale, decisions log 132 ``` 133 134 --- 135 136 ## CSS resolution — why the two producers differ 137 138 The extension never needs a cascade engine of its own — it's already running inside a real, rendered tab, so `getComputedStyle()`/`getBoundingClientRect()` give it ground truth for free. It only needs `core/resolver`'s normalization step and `core/format`; it never even bundles `core/translator`, since capture files are translated later, inside the plugin. 139 140 agent-kit has no browser at all, so it depends on a real one: **Playwright, as a hard dependency from day one** — chosen over a lighter static-parse-first fallback chain for simplicity, and because it's genuinely more capable (true layout for non-auto-layout content and intrinsic sizing that static CSS parsing or jsdom alone can't produce — jsdom resolves cascade but has no layout engine at all). 141 142 --- 143 144 ## MVP roadmap 145 146 | Phase | Focus | Notes | 147 |---|---|---| 148 | P0 | Core engine skeleton | Resolver + Translator as pure, fixture-tested TS packages — no browser or Figma runtime needed to validate the schema. | 149 | P1 | Figma plugin — writer + scraper | Node Writer proven against hand-built fixture files; scraper exports the first real symbol table. Scaffolded on `@create-figma-plugin`. Nothing downstream is useful without this existing first. | 150 | P2 | agent-kit CLI — the flagship | Playwright resolve+translate, tagging convention, published to npm. This is the product most users actually install. | 151 | P3 | Chrome extension | Secondary capture producer, for arbitrary existing pages rather than agent-generated ones. **Scope now includes basic heuristic structural component-matching**, not just value-level token parity (see Decisions — this widens P3 relative to the original cut). | 152 | P4 | Design-system parity depth | Variant-matching precision, fuzzy-match tuning, symbol-table sync UX. Builds on P3's baseline structural matching rather than introducing it. | 153 | P5 | Fidelity polish | Vector-vs-raster handling, font fallback tables, gradients/shadows — effects. Multi-viewport/interactive-state capture is a deliberate boundary, not a queued phase. | 154 155 --- 156 157 ## Foreseeable issues — already designed around 158 159 1. **Schema/version skew.** agent-kit publishes to npm instantly; figma-plugin goes through Figma Community's review, which can lag days. Every file carries an explicit `formatVersion`; the plugin **hard-rejects** a mismatch outright rather than attempting best-effort migration (see Decisions) — fails loudly and specifically rather than misparsing. 160 2. **Fonts, twice over.** `figma.loadFontAsync` only works with fonts the file actually has — needs a fallback-mapping table. Separately, Playwright needs the same webfont to lay out text correctly in the first place, which can fail quietly in a network-restricted agent sandbox. 161 3. **Playwright's footprint in agent sandboxes.** Install/launch friction is a real setup step for agent-kit's actual audience (CI-like, constrained environments) — documented explicitly in the agent guide, not assumed to "just work." 162 4. **Vector vs. raster.** Inline ``/background photographs → image fill. An explicit detection rule, not an accident of whatever's easiest. 163 5. **Scope boundary.** One fixed viewport, one DOM state, per generation. Hover/focus/transitions/breakpoints are out of scope for v1 — stated up front, not discovered later. 164 165 --- 166 167 ## Decisions 168 169 1. **Snippet storage:** plugin-local `clientStorage` only. No sync service. See Snippet storage section above. 170 2. **Fuzzy-match confirmation:** a user-facing plugin setting, not a fixed default. Ships defaulted to auto-bind-on-exact-match, with a toggle for confirm-everything. See Node Writer section above. 171 3. **Component-level detection for arbitrary captured pages:** included in v1 (P3), not deferred. The Chrome extension ships with basic heuristic structural component-matching alongside value-level token parity. This widens P3's scope versus the original value-only cut, and P4 now builds on that baseline rather than introducing structural matching itself. 172 4. **Format-version policy:** hard-reject a `formatVersion` mismatch outright. No best-effort backward migration. Simpler, safer, and consistent with "fails loudly and specifically" in Foreseeable Issues. 173 5. **Plugin build tooling:** adopt `@create-figma-plugin` for the `figma-plugin` package rather than a hand-rolled build. Saves setup time for P1 and is the widely-used convention for Figma plugin tooling. 174 175 --- 176 177 *flowfig · architecture plan · updated 2026-07-18 · decisions resolved, repo scaffolding starting* 178
